title: Default Alive or Default Dead
body:
# Default Alive or Default Dead

A diagnostic framework for startup survival introduced by Paul Graham. The question: assuming expenses stay constant and revenue grows at its current trend, will the startup reach profitability on the money it has? ([Default alive or default dead](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/95-default-alive-or-default-dead))

## The Framework

**Default alive:** Revenue [[Growth|growth]] will cover expenses before money runs out. The company survives even if it never raises again.

**Default dead:** Expenses exceed what revenue growth can cover in the available time. The company must either raise more money (see [[Fundraising]]) or cut costs.

Graham's alarming claim: half the founders he talks to do not know which category they are in ([Default alive or default dead](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/95-default-alive-or-default-dead)). This ignorance is a core [[Financial Survival]] failure mode.

## The Fatal Pinch

The worst state: default dead, with slow [[Growth|growth]], and not enough time or money to fix either. By the time founders recognize a fatal pinch, options have narrowed to near-zero ([Default alive or default dead](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/95-default-alive-or-default-dead)).

## Plan A / Plan B

Graham recommends maintaining two plans:

**Plan A:** The growth plan — [[Hiring|hire]], spend, scale. This is the default mode when things are going well.

**Plan B:** The survival plan — cut to a skeleton crew, reach ramen profitability. This plan should be written down in advance, with a specific date by which the founder switches to it if Plan A is not working ([Default alive or default dead](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/95-default-alive-or-default-dead)).

Having Plan B written down is what distinguishes careful founders from reckless ones. The plan itself may never be executed, but the act of writing it forces the founder to understand their real financial position. This ties directly to the [[Founder Mindset]] of operating with clear-eyed realism about [[Financial Survival]].

## Source Talks

| Source | Speaker | Key Point |
|--------|---------|-----------|
| [Default alive or default dead](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/95-default-alive-or-default-dead) | Paul Graham | The complete framework |

title: Do Things That Don't Scale
body:
# Do Things That Don't Scale

One of Paul Graham's most influential essays and a foundational YC principle. The core claim: startups take off because founders make them take off through manual, unscalable work. There is no shortcut ([Do things that don't scale](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/96-do-things-that-don-t-scale)).

## The Framework

A [[Startup Ideas|startup idea]] is not just "what you build." Graham reframes it as a vector — a pair of (what you build) + (the unscalable thing you do to get it going). Both components matter ([Do things that don't scale](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/96-do-things-that-don-t-scale)).

## Named Patterns

**The Collison Installation.** Named after Stripe's Patrick and John Collison. When someone agreed to try Stripe, instead of emailing a signup link, the brothers said "give me your laptop" and set them up on the spot. The lesson: be aggressive about onboarding. Do not let friction between "yes" and "using it" eat your conversion ([Do things that don't scale](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/96-do-things-that-don-t-scale)). This is a canonical [[Early-Stage Tactics|early-stage tactic]].

**The Contained Fire.** Focus on a deliberately narrow market and make it white-hot. Facebook launched at Harvard only. The small market lets founders give extraordinary attention to every user, and word-of-mouth spreads faster in a dense community. This pattern directly enables [[Growth|growth]] through concentration ([Do things that don't scale](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/96-do-things-that-don-t-scale)).

**Pulling a Meraki.** Named after Meraki (YC W06), who assembled their first routers by hand. Hardware startups can do manufacturing themselves initially, using the low-volume phase to learn what works before investing in factory production. This is a [[Product Development|product development]] insight as much as a sales one ([Do things that don't scale](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/96-do-things-that-don-t-scale)).

**The Consulting Model.** Pick one user and build as if you are their personal consultant. The custom work reveals what the product should become — the mold produces a reusable shape. This approach connects to [[Sales and Distribution|sales]] at the earliest stage ([Do things that don't scale](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/96-do-things-that-don-t-scale)).

## When It Stops Applying

Graham is clear that unscalable tactics are for the early stage. They are the crank that starts the engine. Once the engine is running (the startup has genuine [[Growth|product-market fit]] and organic growth), the focus shifts to scaling. But founders consistently err on the side of trying to scale too early, not too late ([Do things that don't scale](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/96-do-things-that-don-t-scale)).

## Source Talks

| Source | Speaker | Key Point |
|--------|---------|-----------|
| [Do things that don't scale](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/96-do-things-that-don-t-scale) | Paul Graham | The essay defining this framework |

title: Hiring
body:
# Hiring

Hiring is where many startups first encounter the tension between [[Growth]] ambition and [[Financial Survival|survival]]. Graham calls hiring too fast "by far the biggest killer of startups that raise money" ([Default alive or default dead](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/95-default-alive-or-default-dead)). The YC canon treats hiring as a high-stakes decision that should be approached with caution, not speed.

## When to Hire

Graham argues that founders often hire to solve slow [[Growth|growth]], when the real problem is that the [[Product Development|product]] is not appealing enough. "There is surprisingly little connection between how much a startup spends and how fast it grows" ([Default alive or default dead](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/95-default-alive-or-default-dead)). He cites Airbnb, which waited four months after [[Fundraising|raising money]] before hiring employee #1.

The right time to hire is when there is clearly more work than the founders can do and the type of work is well-understood. Hiring into ambiguity — before the founders know what needs to be built — creates more problems than it solves. This aligns with the [[Do Things That Don't Scale]] principle: founders should do the work themselves first to understand it deeply.

## Your First Engineering Hire

Taggar recommends focusing the first three engineering hires exclusively on personal network hiring ([How to hire your first engineer](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4H-how-to-hire-your-first-engineer)). His 7-step pipeline: list everyone you know who codes, invite them to try your product, ask if they would consider joining, follow up, ask for introductions to engineers they respect, and repeat.

He frames hiring as [[Sales and Distribution|sales]]: "Asking anyone to join as your first hire is asking for an order of magnitude greater commitment than pitching an investor to invest" ([Convincing engineers to join your team](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4G-convincing-engineers-to-join-your-team)). The pitch to candidates is fundamentally different from the pitch to investors — candidates care about [[Growth|growth]] opportunity, technical challenge, and the team they will work with, not market size.

Taggar ranks hiring channels in order of effectiveness: personal networks, marketplaces (Triplebyte, AngelList), inbound applications, cold outreach, recruiters, meetups, agencies.

## Hire for Hunger

Epstein argues in "Advice for New Managers" that startups should hire for hunger — passion for the mission will drive more long-term impact than any specific skill set ([Advice for new managers](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/2s-advice-for-new-managers)). The "hire slow, fire fast" principle applies: take time to find the right person, but act quickly when a hire is not working. This principle also applies to choosing [[Cofounders]] — look for commitment and drive over credentials.

## The Danger of Overhiring

Graham's [[Default Alive or Default Dead]] essay makes the case most forcefully: hiring is the primary way startups burn through their [[Financial Survival|runway]] without improving their trajectory ([Default alive or default dead](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/95-default-alive-or-default-dead)). Each hire increases [[Financial Survival|burn rate]] but may not increase [[Growth|growth]]. Founders should model their path to profitability with and without each hire.

In "The 18 Mistakes," Graham lists hiring bad programmers (#6) and spending too much (#12) — both of which frequently manifest as overhiring ([The 18 mistakes that kill startups](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8t-the-18-mistakes-that-kill-startups)).

## Source Talks

| Source | Speaker | Key Point | URL |
|--------|---------|-----------|-----|
| 4H-how-to-hire-your-first-engineer | Harj Taggar | Personal network pipeline, hiring as sales, channel ranking | https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4H-how-to-hire-your-first-engineer |
| 4G-convincing-engineers-to-join-your-team | Harj Taggar | Pitching to candidates, what engineers care about | https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4G-convincing-engineers-to-join-your-team |
| 95-default-alive-or-default-dead | Paul Graham | Overhiring as the #1 killer of funded startups | https://www.ycombinator.com/library/95-default-alive-or-default-dead |
| 8t-the-18-mistakes-that-kill-startups | Paul Graham | Hiring bad programmers, spending too much | https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8t-the-18-mistakes-that-kill-startups |
| 2s-advice-for-new-managers | Aaron Epstein | Hire for hunger, hire slow fire fast | https://www.ycombinator.com/library/2s-advice-for-new-managers |

title: Management
body:
# Management

The transition from individual contributor to manager is one of the most difficult adjustments in a startup. Epstein argues that founders must "create both a product that people love and a company where people want to work at the same time" ([Advice for new managers](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/2s-advice-for-new-managers)).

## The Maker-to-Manager Transition

The most common failure mode: an outstanding individual contributor becomes a manager and tries to do both jobs. Epstein calls this the "150% workload" problem — the new manager takes on management responsibilities while continuing to do their old job, resulting in less than 100% output in either area ([Advice for new managers](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/2s-advice-for-new-managers)).

The fix is genuine delegation. Epstein advises: "Focus on the What, not the How." Tell people what needs to be accomplished, not how to do it. This is uncomfortable for technical founders who are used to being the best executor in the room. The shift mirrors the broader [[Founder Mindset]] challenge of letting go of the work that got you here.

## Core Principles

**Deflect credit, absorb blame.** When things go well, point to the team. When things go wrong, take responsibility. This builds trust and psychological safety.

**Hire for hunger.** Passion for the mission drives more long-term impact than specific skills. People who are hungry will learn what they need to learn. See [[Hiring]] for the full framework on when and how to hire.

**Hire slow, fire fast.** Take time to find the right person. Act quickly when a hire is not working — the longer a bad fit stays, the more damage it does to the team ([Advice for new managers](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/2s-advice-for-new-managers)).

**People leave managers, not companies.** The quality of the direct manager relationship is the strongest predictor of retention. This is critical for startups where every departure has outsized impact on [[Financial Survival|burn rate]] and team capacity.

## One-on-Ones

Epstein treats 1:1s as the most important recurring commitment for a manager ([Advice for new managers](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/2s-advice-for-new-managers)). They are the primary mechanism for understanding what each person needs, catching problems early, and building the trust that makes delegation possible. For [[Cofounders]], a similar pattern of regular check-ins (called "founder syncs") helps surface tensions before they become breakups.

## The Second Job of a Startup CEO

As the company scales past the [[Early-Stage Tactics|early stage]], the CEO's role shifts from building the [[Product Development|product]] to building the company ([The second job of a startup CEO](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/3k-the-second-job-of-a-startup-ceo)). This includes [[Hiring]], [[Fundraising]], setting culture, and managing the [[Board Governance|board]]. The transition is disorienting for technical founders whose identity is tied to making things.

## Source Talks

| Source | Speaker | Key Point | URL |
|--------|---------|-----------|-----|
| 2s-advice-for-new-managers | Aaron Epstein | Maker-to-manager, delegation, hire for hunger, 1:1s | https://www.ycombinator.com/library/2s-advice-for-new-managers |
| 3j-how-to-measure-leadership | Y Combinator | Leadership measurement frameworks | https://www.ycombinator.com/library/3j-how-to-measure-leadership |
| 3k-the-second-job-of-a-startup-ceo | Y Combinator | CEO role evolution as company scales | https://www.ycombinator.com/library/3k-the-second-job-of-a-startup-ceo |

title: Founder Mindset
body:
# Founder Mindset

Graham argues that the most important quality in a startup founder is determination. Not intelligence — determination ([The Hardest Lessons for Startups to Learn](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8w-the-hardest-lessons-for-startups-to-learn)). Across several essays, he builds a picture of the founder mindset as a specific combination of relentlessness and flexibility.

## Relentlessly Resourceful

In the essay of that title, Graham proposes "relentlessly resourceful" as the two-word answer to what makes a good founder ([Be Relentlessly Resourceful](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/94-be-relentlessly-resourceful)). Not merely relentless — that produces stubbornness. Not merely resourceful — that produces aimlessness. The combination is what matters.

He uses the running back metaphor: a good running back does not just run straight ahead (relentless) or dance around (resourceful). They do both — pushing forward while constantly adapting to the field. This same metaphor appears in "The Hardest Lessons."

Graham writes that "Make something people want" is the destination, but "Be relentlessly resourceful" is how you get there. He also argues this quality can be taught — "surprisingly often it can. Not to everyone, but to many people." This resourcefulness is what enables the [[Early-Stage Tactics]] of doing things that don't scale.

## Counterintuitive Nature of Startups

"Before the Startup" identifies six ways startups are counterintuitive ([Before the Startup](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8y-before-the-startup)). The most important: the way to succeed is not to be an expert on startups but to be an expert on users and the problem. "Starting a startup is where gaming the system stops working. There is no boss to trick, only users."

Graham warns specifically about "playing house" — going through the motions of having a startup (office, business cards, [[Hiring]]) without doing the hard work of making something people want. This connects to his advice in [[Startup Ideas]] that domain expertise matters more than startup mechanics.

## What Actually Kills Startups

Graham's "18 Mistakes" identifies the single meta-mistake: not making something users want ([The 18 Mistakes That Kill Startups](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8t-the-18-mistakes-that-kill-startups)). "Nearly all failure funnels through that." The most common form of startup death is not a spectacular flameout but a half-hearted side project that never gets anywhere (#18: half-hearted effort).

In "The Hardest Lessons," he identifies three self-inflicted causes of death: internal disputes between [[Cofounders]], inertia (not releasing, not iterating), and ignoring users ([The Hardest Lessons for Startups to Learn](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8w-the-hardest-lessons-for-startups-to-learn)). Competitors almost never kill startups — startups kill themselves.

## Optimism and Deals

Graham treats founder optimism as a nuclear reactor: enormously powerful but dangerous if unshielded. Founders should be optimistic about their ability to build something great, but cynical about deals, partnerships, and anything that requires other people to follow through ([The Hardest Lessons for Startups to Learn](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8w-the-hardest-lessons-for-startups-to-learn)). "The most dangerous thing is to let them go to your head." This skepticism is especially important during [[Fundraising]] and [[Sales and Distribution]] negotiations.

## Formidable

In the [[Fundraising]] context, Graham describes the quality investors look for as "formidable" — not just confident, but justifiably confident ([How to Convince Investors](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/98-how-to-convince-investors)). The best way for inexperienced founders to be formidable is to stick to the truth about what they know and what they have built.

## Source Talks

| Source | Speaker | Key Point |
|--------|---------|-----------|
| [94-be-relentlessly-resourceful](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/94-be-relentlessly-resourceful) | Paul Graham | Relentlessly resourceful as the defining founder quality |
| [8w-the-hardest-lessons-for-startups-to-learn](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8w-the-hardest-lessons-for-startups-to-learn) | Paul Graham | Determination > intelligence; running back metaphor; three killers |
| [8y-before-the-startup](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8y-before-the-startup) | Paul Graham | Counterintuitive nature; playing house; domain expertise > startup expertise |
| [8t-the-18-mistakes-that-kill-startups](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8t-the-18-mistakes-that-kill-startups) | Paul Graham | The meta-mistake; half-hearted effort as the most common death |
| [98-how-to-convince-investors](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/98-how-to-convince-investors) | Paul Graham | Formidable = justifiably confident |

title: Board Governance
body:
# Board Governance

## Board Meetings

Hariharan argues that the biggest mistake is treating board meetings as status updates. Best CEOs spend only the first 45 minutes on KPIs and allocate the majority of time to 1-2 strategic deep-dives on the hardest problems ([How to create and manage a board](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/3w-how-to-create-and-manage-a-board)).

Written memos (pioneered by companies like Segment and Faire) are superior to slides for strategic board discussion. They force clearer thinking and allow board members to engage with nuance rather than reacting to bullet points ([How to create and manage a board](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/3w-how-to-create-and-manage-a-board)).

## Building Board Relationships

The #1 attribute needed in a board member is trust, and the only way to build it is time. Hariharan recommends getting to know VC partners 6-9 months before [[Fundraising|fundraising]] begins ([How to create and manage a board](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/3w-how-to-create-and-manage-a-board)).

Monthly 2-page update emails accomplish three things: board members can help with specific asks, quarterly meetings are better-prepared, and the CEO gains a forcing function for reflection. This practice ties into broader [[Management|management]] discipline.

## Board Control

Kwon and Harris warn that the most significant way founders lose control at Series A is the 2-2-1 board structure (2 founders, 2 investors, 1 independent) — because it means founders can be fired from their own company. A 2-1 structure preserves founder control ([A standard and clean Series A term sheet](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4P-a-standard-and-clean-series-a-term-sheet)).

"When an investor says that they're committed to partnering with you for the long-term but then tells you something else with the terms they insist on, believe the terms." Series A documents set precedent for future rounds — bad terms compound and are difficult to unwind ([A standard and clean Series A term sheet](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4P-a-standard-and-clean-series-a-term-sheet)). This connects directly to [[Financial Survival]] and long-term [[Fundraising]] strategy.

## Source Talks

| Source | Speaker | Key Point |
|--------|---------|-----------|
| [How to create and manage a board](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/3w-how-to-create-and-manage-a-board) | Anu Hariharan | Board meetings as strategy sessions, written memos, monthly updates |
| [A standard and clean Series A term sheet](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4P-a-standard-and-clean-series-a-term-sheet) | Jason Kwon, Aaron Harris | Board control, clean vs. dirty terms, believe the terms |

title: Sales and Distribution
body:
# Sales and Distribution

## Enterprise Sales

Junee reframes enterprise sales as hacking a complex system. "A large enterprise is a complex network of people and processes — and it can be understood and influenced in the same way" as a computer system ([Enterprise sales for hackers](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4m-enterprise-sales-for-hackers)). The best [[Early-Stage Tactics|early-stage]] enterprise sellers are hackers who learn the system, not "script kiddies" running a repeatable playbook.

**Stakeholder mapping** is essential. Each enterprise deal involves a champion (your internal advocate), detractors, IT, procurement, legal, and finance — each with distinct incentives. Champions are gold: Junee advises building relationships deep enough that you can text them any time to revive a stalled deal ([Enterprise sales for hackers](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4m-enterprise-sales-for-hackers)).

**[[Hiring]] salespeople:** match to your company stage. "Coin-operated" reps from big companies are the wrong fit at early stage. You need people comfortable with uncertainty who can learn the system ([Enterprise sales for hackers](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4m-enterprise-sales-for-hackers)).

## Press and PR

Seibel argues that founders should build reporter relationships directly. "Reporters want to talk to CEOs and [[Cofounders|co-founders]] — not PR people." His own company wasted over $100K on PR agencies before learning this ([Getting press for your startup](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4c-getting-press-for-your-startup)).

**Press cadence** beats big splashes. One story every 1-2 months is more effective than one mega-story per year. Maintain a queue of 3-5 upcoming news items. Seibel identifies four categories of startup news: [[Product Development|product]] launches, milestones, significant BD deals, and [[Fundraising|fundraising]] ([Getting press for your startup](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4c-getting-press-for-your-startup)).

**Offer exclusives.** Almost all startups should pitch their story to one reporter at a time rather than blasting many outlets. PR should consume no more than 5% of the CEO's time, and benefits are typically delayed by weeks or months ([Getting press for your startup](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4c-getting-press-for-your-startup)).

## Source Talks

| Source | Speaker | Key Point |
|--------|---------|-----------|
| [Enterprise sales for hackers](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4m-enterprise-sales-for-hackers) | Ryan Junee | Enterprise as hackable system, stakeholder mapping, champion relationships |
| [Getting press for your startup](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4c-getting-press-for-your-startup) | Michael Seibel | PR like biz dev, cadence over splash, founder-led media relations |

title: Financial Survival
body:
# Financial Survival

Graham claims that half the founders he talks to do not know whether their company is [[Default Alive or Default Dead|default alive or default dead]]. He considers this the single most important question for any startup past roughly 9 months old ([Default alive or default dead](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/95-default-alive-or-default-dead)).

## Default Alive or Default Dead

The [[Default Alive or Default Dead]] framework is simple: given current expenses and current revenue [[Growth|growth]] trend, will the startup reach profitability on the money it has left? If yes, the company is default alive. If no, it is default dead.

Default dead does not mean the company will die — it means the company must either raise more money (see [[Fundraising]]) or cut costs. But founders who do not know their status cannot make informed decisions about [[Hiring]], spending, or [[Fundraising|fundraising timing]].

## The Fatal Pinch

The worst position is what Graham calls the fatal pinch: default dead, with slow [[Growth|growth]], and not enough time or money to fix either ([Default alive or default dead](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/95-default-alive-or-default-dead)). By the time founders recognize they are in a fatal pinch, it is often too late. The solution is to monitor the default alive/dead status continuously and act early.

## Plan A / Plan B

Graham recommends always having a written-down plan for surviving if the next fundraise fails ([Default alive or default dead](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/95-default-alive-or-default-dead)). This is Plan B — typically cutting to a skeleton crew and reaching ramen profitability. The plan should include a specific date by which the founder switches from Plan A ([[Growth|growth]] mode) to Plan B (survival mode) if [[Fundraising|fundraising]] has not succeeded.

## Premature Scaling

Graham attributes to Paul Buchheit the observation that many startups scale a business with bad unit economics to chase impressive [[Growth|growth]] numbers ([Default alive or default dead](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/95-default-alive-or-default-dead)). This "premature scaling" accelerates burn without improving the underlying business. This connects to [[Do Things That Don't Scale]] — founders should focus on getting the unit economics right before automating and scaling.

## The Hiring Trap

[[Hiring]] too fast is "by far the biggest killer of startups that raise money" ([Default alive or default dead](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/95-default-alive-or-default-dead)). Founders hire to solve slow [[Growth|growth]], but the real problem is usually that the [[Product Development|product]] is not good enough. Each hire increases burn rate. Graham argues there is "surprisingly little connection between how much a startup spends and how fast it grows."

Airbnb waited four months after [[Fundraising|raising]] before making their first hire. The founders did all the work themselves until they were certain what needed to be done and that they could not do it alone — a pattern consistent with [[Early-Stage Tactics]] and [[Do Things That Don't Scale]].

## Source Talks

| Source | Speaker | Key Point | URL |
|--------|---------|-----------|-----|
| 95-default-alive-or-default-dead | Paul Graham | Default alive/dead framework, fatal pinch, Plan A/B, hiring trap | https://www.ycombinator.com/library/95-default-alive-or-default-dead |
| 8t-the-18-mistakes-that-kill-startups | Paul Graham | Raising too little (#11), spending too much (#12), raising too much (#13) | https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8t-the-18-mistakes-that-kill-startups |
| 3Z-advice-for-companies-with-less-than-1-year-of-runway | Y Combinator | Practical advice for low-runway situations | https://www.ycombinator.com/library/3Z-advice-for-companies-with-less-than-1-year-of-runway |

title: Product Development
body:
# Product Development

The YC canon on product development is dominated by one principle: release early and iterate. Graham writes, "I've seen a lot of startups die because they were too slow to release stuff, and none because they were too quick" ([The Hardest Lessons for Startups to Learn](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8w-the-hardest-lessons-for-startups-to-learn)).

## Release Early, Iterate Fast

Graham argues that the primary job of a startup is to pump out features, collect user feedback, and iterate ([The Hardest Lessons for Startups to Learn](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8w-the-hardest-lessons-for-startups-to-learn)). Waiting for perfection is a form of procrastination. The product will always feel unfinished — the question is whether users find it useful enough to keep coming back. This connects directly to the [[Early-Stage Tactics]] principle that startups die from slowness, not speed.

## The Two-Week Cycle

Shear describes the development process at Twitch (then Justin.tv): strict two-week cycles where no item could last into the next cycle ([How to Prioritize Features](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8p-how-to-prioritize-features)). If a feature was too large, it was broken into smaller chunks. This created constant shipping cadence and prevented scope creep.

Within each cycle, the team set three goals (e.g., increase content creation, acquire new users, improve retention) and selected features that mapped to those goals. Features were graded Easy/Medium/Hard for engineering effort, and the team used consensus-based prioritization.

## Analytics-First

Shear argues that no feature should be released without releasing the analytics for it ([How to Prioritize Features](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8p-how-to-prioritize-features)). Before building, the team defined the specific measurable result they wanted. This prevented building features that "felt right" but had no impact. Measurable outcomes tie directly to [[Growth]] metrics — weekly growth rate serves as the ultimate arbiter of whether a feature mattered.

## Need-to-Have vs. Nice-to-Have

Shear separated feature requests into need-to-have and nice-to-have ([How to Prioritize Features](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8p-how-to-prioritize-features)). Non-technical team members, once they understood engineering effort estimates, "often got better at thinking up easier and easier MVPs of their ideas." This pushed the team toward simpler solutions that delivered the core value.

## No Roadmap Beyond the Current Cycle

Shear's team maintained no roadmap beyond the current two-week cycle ([How to Prioritize Features](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8p-how-to-prioritize-features)). This kept the team responsive to user feedback and prevented committing to features that might not matter by the time they were built. This approach aligns with the [[Founder Mindset]] of staying close to users rather than planning in the abstract, and with the [[Do Things That Don't Scale]] framework of learning through direct engagement.

## Source Talks

| Source | Speaker | Key Point |
|--------|---------|-----------|
| [8p-how-to-prioritize-features](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8p-how-to-prioritize-features) | Emmett Shear | Two-week cycles, three goals, Easy/Medium/Hard, analytics-first |
| [8w-the-hardest-lessons-for-startups-to-learn](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8w-the-hardest-lessons-for-startups-to-learn) | Paul Graham | Release early; pump out features; don't wait for perfection |
| [4e-guide-to-product-development](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4e-guide-to-product-development) | Y Combinator | Product development principles |

title: Growth
body:
# Growth

Graham defines a startup as a company designed to grow fast ([Startup = Growth](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8s-startup-growth)). Everything else about startups — the [[Fundraising]], the exits, the culture — follows from this single characteristic. "Startup = Growth" is the foundational equation.

## Growth as Compass

Graham argues that every founder should know their growth rate at all times, and that it should be measured as a ratio (percentage), not an absolute number ([Startup = Growth](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8s-startup-growth)). A constant number of new customers per month means the growth rate is actually decreasing.

Picking a weekly growth rate target and optimizing for it turns the bewilderingly complex problem of running a startup into a single optimization problem. During YC, 5-7% weekly growth is good. 10% is exceptional. 1% is a sign something is wrong.

## The Math of Compound Growth

Small differences in weekly growth rate produce qualitatively different outcomes:
- 1% weekly = 1.7x per year
- 5% weekly = 12.6x per year
- 10% weekly = 142x per year

This exponential math is why the startup ecosystem (VCs, acquisitions, high failure rates) exists at all. It is why investors think in power laws, and why a startup that compounds at 5% weekly for 4 years reaches a scale that linear businesses never approach. This math also explains why [[Early-Stage Tactics]] like the [[Do Things That Don't Scale]] approach matter — even small initial user bases compound into large numbers.

## The S-Curve

Growth has three phases ([Startup = Growth](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8s-startup-growth)). The first phase is slow — the founders are figuring out what they are doing. The second phase is rapid growth, which is the defining period. The third phase is maturity, when growth slows as the company approaches the limits of its market.

## Growth as Evolutionary Pressure

Focusing on a weekly growth target does more than measure progress. Graham argues it actually discovers better [[Startup Ideas]] through evolutionary pressure ([Startup = Growth](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8s-startup-growth)). When founders commit to hitting a growth number every week, they are forced to find the tactics that work and discard those that do not. The idea itself evolves toward product-market fit.

## When Growth Is Absent

In "[[Default Alive or Default Dead]]," Graham introduces a simple diagnostic: given current expenses and current revenue growth, will the startup reach profitability on the money it has? If not, the company is "default dead," and the founders need either faster growth or lower burn ([Default Alive or Default Dead](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/95-default-alive-or-default-dead)).

He argues that there is "surprisingly little connection between how much a startup spends and how fast it grows." Fast growth comes from [[Product Development]] that achieves product-market fit, not from spending. [[Hiring]] more people to solve a growth problem usually makes things worse — a point connected to [[Financial Survival]] and burn rate discipline.

## Source Talks

| Source | Speaker | Key Point |
|--------|---------|-----------|
| [8s-startup-growth](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8s-startup-growth) | Paul Graham | Startup = Growth; weekly rate as compass; S-curve; compound math |
| [95-default-alive-or-default-dead](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/95-default-alive-or-default-dead) | Paul Graham | Growth trend as input to alive/dead calculation |
| [96-do-things-that-don-t-scale](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/96-do-things-that-don-t-scale) | Paul Graham | Compound growth from small initial user bases |
| [8w-the-hardest-lessons-for-startups-to-learn](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8w-the-hardest-lessons-for-startups-to-learn) | Paul Graham | Release early to start the growth clock |

title: Cofounders
body:
# Cofounders

YC data shows that only 7.9% of YC's top companies were founded by solo founders. Graham lists "single founder" as mistake #1 in his "18 Mistakes" essay ([The 18 mistakes that kill startups](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8t-the-18-mistakes-that-kill-startups)). The canon is clear: cofounders matter, and finding the right one is among the most consequential decisions a founder makes.

## Where to Find Cofounders

Graham and Seibel both argue that school is by far the best place to find cofounders ([A student's guide to startups](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/93-a-student-s-guide-to-startups)). The window closes quickly after graduation — the density of ambitious, available, risk-tolerant people drops sharply once people enter full-time jobs.

YC's cofounder matching platform has produced successful results. Sequin and Kiwi Biosciences both met through the platform and were accepted into YC S21. Li describes a "trial project approach" where matched cofounders work together on a real project before committing ([Does co-founder matching work](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/Cq-does-co-founder-matching-work)). This mirrors [[Early-Stage Tactics]] — test before you commit.

## Evaluating Compatibility

YC recommends discussing 10 key questions before committing: personal goals, roles and CEO designation, equity split, location, pivot willingness, full-time triggers, personal finances, working schedule, team culture vision, and disagreement resolution ([10 questions to discuss with a potential co-founder](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/Hj-10-questions-to-discuss-with-a-potential-co-founder)).

Kiwi Biosciences took a "very structured approach" — answering all 50 questions from First Round's cofounder questionnaire and finding deep alignment on conflict resolution and culture before committing. YC advises: "No questionnaire can be a substitute for actually spending a lot of time together in person. It usually takes several months."

Amy Buechler recommends "founder syncs" — recurring weekly 1-on-1s specifically about how the cofounder relationship is going, designed to surface tensions before they become breakups. This is analogous to the 1:1 practice in [[Management]].

## Equity and Roles

The equity split should reflect expected future contributions over the 7-10 year marathon ahead, not past contributions. This is especially important for scientific founders who may feel their prior research entitles them to a larger share.

Deciding who is CEO early prevents ambiguity. YC advises treating cofounder selection with the same rigor as [[Hiring]] — including reference calls on each other ([10 questions to discuss with a potential co-founder](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/Hj-10-questions-to-discuss-with-a-potential-co-founder)).

## What Kills Cofounder Relationships

Graham identifies fights between founders as mistake #17 ([The 18 mistakes that kill startups](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8t-the-18-mistakes-that-kill-startups)). In "The Hardest Lessons," he names internal disputes as one of three self-inflicted causes of startup death (alongside inertia and ignoring users). Cofounder conflict is one of the most common reasons startups fail before they run out of money — making it a [[Financial Survival]] threat that does not show up on a balance sheet.

A strong cofounder relationship also supports the [[Founder Mindset]] required to endure the years of uncertainty that building a startup demands. Graham argues in "Why to not not start a startup" that having a cofounder makes the emotional burden bearable ([Why to not not start a startup](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8x-why-to-not-not-start-a-startup)).

## Source Talks

| Cq-does-co-founder-matching-work | Catheryn Li | YC matching platform success stories, trial project approach | https://www.ycombinator.com/library/Cq-does-co-founder-matching-work |
| 8t-the-18-mistakes-that-kill-startups | Paul Graham | Solo founder (#1) and founder fights (#17) as top mistakes | https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8t-the-18-mistakes-that-kill-startups |
| 93-a-student-s-guide-to-startups | Paul Graham | School as the best cofounder-finding environment | https://www.ycombinator.com/library/93-a-student-s-guide-to-startups |

title: Fundraising
body:
# Fundraising

Fundraising is one of the most written-about topics in the YC library, covered from seed mechanics to Series A diligence to the psychology of convincing investors.

## When to Raise

Graham argues that the time to raise money is not when you need it — it is when you can convince investors ([How to convince investors](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/98-how-to-convince-investors)). Fundraising when desperate is a losing position. Ralston recommends raising seed when the startup has a story that is "interesting enough to get an investor to open their checkbook" ([A guide to seed fundraising](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4A-a-guide-to-seed-fundraising)). Startups that are [[Financial Survival|default dead]] face a weaker negotiating position when approaching investors.

## How Much to Raise (Seed)

Ralston's "Guide to Seed Fundraising" recommends raising enough to get to the next milestone, typically 12-18 months of runway ([A guide to seed fundraising](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4A-a-guide-to-seed-fundraising)). The amount depends on the type of business. In Graham's framing, both raising too little (#11 in "18 Mistakes") and raising too much (#13) are dangerous — too little leaves no margin for error, too much creates complacency and overspending ([The 18 mistakes that kill startups](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8t-the-18-mistakes-that-kill-startups)). How much to raise is closely tied to [[Financial Survival]] — founders should model their [[Financial Survival|burn rate]] and path to [[Default Alive or Default Dead|default alive]] status.

## Financing Instruments

Ralston covers three main options at seed ([A guide to seed fundraising](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4A-a-guide-to-seed-fundraising)):

**SAFE (Simple Agreement for Future Equity).** YC's preferred instrument. Simpler than convertible notes, no interest, no maturity date. Converts to equity at the next priced round.

**Convertible Notes.** Debt that converts to equity. Simpler than a priced round but more complex than SAFEs. Include interest and maturity dates.

**Priced Equity Round.** Standard stock purchase. More expensive and complex at seed stage. More common for larger seed rounds.

## Convincing Investors

Graham identifies three things investors need to see: formidable founders, a promising market, and evidence of success so far ([How to convince investors](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/98-how-to-convince-investors)).

**Seeming formidable.** Not just confident — justifiably confident. Graham argues the best way for inexperienced founders to do this is to stick to the truth. "Convince yourself first, then explain." Investors can sense the difference between genuine conviction from domain expertise and the Dunning-Kruger effect. This connects to [[Founder Mindset]] — the conviction required to fundraise is the same conviction required to build.

**Market sizing.** Investors think in markets, founders think in [[Startup Ideas|ideas]]. Framing the opportunity as a large addressable market, and answering "why now?" (what changed to make this possible), are essential.

**Handling rejection.** Graham recommends being candid about why investors turned you down and why they are mistaken. The best investors already believe most other investors are conventional-minded.

## Pitch Decks

Hale argues that a pitch deck should answer three questions: What do you do? How big is the market? What is your traction? He recommends keeping decks to 10-15 slides and leading with the clearest possible statement of the problem and solution ([How to design a better pitch deck](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4T-how-to-design-a-better-pitch-deck)).

Tam's Series A deck guide emphasizes that Series A pitches must show metrics and traction, not just vision. The shift from seed to Series A is from "promising" to "delivering" ([How to build a great Series A pitch and deck](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8d-how-to-build-a-great-series-a-pitch-and-deck)). [[Growth]] metrics — revenue, engagement, retention — become the centerpiece.

## Series A Mechanics

Harris's diligence checklist covers what founders need to have ready: corporate records, business plan and financials, IP documentation, securities, material agreements, and employee/benefits records ([Series A diligence checklist](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/3h-series-a-diligence-checklist)). Having a complete data room before signing a term sheet can cut a week off closing.

A critical detail: all pending equity grants should be completed before starting the Series A process. Once a term sheet exists, the old 409A valuation is no longer usable, which hurts employees hired under the prior valuation. This intersects with [[Hiring]] — founders should plan equity grants relative to fundraising timelines.

## Source Talks

|--------|---------|-----------|-----|
| 4A-a-guide-to-seed-fundraising | Geoff Ralston | Comprehensive seed mechanics: SAFEs, convertible notes, how much to raise | https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4A-a-guide-to-seed-fundraising |
| 98-how-to-convince-investors | Paul Graham | Formidable founders, convince yourself first, handling rejection | https://www.ycombinator.com/library/98-how-to-convince-investors |
| 4T-how-to-design-a-better-pitch-deck | Kevin Hale | Pitch deck structure and design principles | https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4T-how-to-design-a-better-pitch-deck |
| 8d-how-to-build-a-great-series-a-pitch-and-deck | Janelle Tam | Series A deck: metrics-first, traction over vision | https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8d-how-to-build-a-great-series-a-pitch-and-deck |
| 3h-series-a-diligence-checklist | Aaron Harris | Data room preparation, 409A timing, closing process | https://www.ycombinator.com/library/3h-series-a-diligence-checklist |
| 8t-the-18-mistakes-that-kill-startups | Paul Graham | Raising too little, too much, poor investor management | https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8t-the-18-mistakes-that-kill-startups |

title: Early-Stage Tactics
body:
# Early-Stage Tactics

Startups take off because founders make them take off. Graham uses the metaphor of a car crank — a separate, laborious process is needed to get the engine going ([Do Things That Don't Scale](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/96-do-things-that-don-t-scale)). There is no passive "if you build it, they will come."

## Recruit Users Manually

Nearly all startups must recruit their first users by hand. Graham cites Stripe as the canonical example ([Do Things That Don't Scale](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/96-do-things-that-don-t-scale)). The Collison brothers invented what YC calls the "Collison Installation": when someone agreed to try Stripe, instead of sending a link, they said "give me your laptop" and set them up on the spot.

Founders resist manual user acquisition for two reasons: shyness (they would rather write code than talk to strangers) and disbelief (the absolute numbers seem too small to matter). Both are wrong. The power of compound [[Growth]] means even 10 users growing at 10% weekly produces 14,000 users in a year.

## Delight Early Users

Graham argues that founders should take extraordinary measures to make early users happy ([Do Things That Don't Scale](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/96-do-things-that-don-t-scale)). The goal is not to build an "insanely great" product yet — at the larval stage, it is the experience of being a user that should be insanely great.

He writes that he has "never once seen a startup lured down a blind alley by trying too hard to make their initial users happy." Over-engaging with early users is both a [[Growth]] tactic and a [[Product Development]] strategy — the feedback loop makes the product better.

## The Contained Fire

Focus on a deliberately narrow market first and make it white-hot before expanding. Facebook launched at Harvard only. Once it dominated there, it expanded to other schools. Graham calls this strategy the "contained fire" ([Do Things That Don't Scale](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/96-do-things-that-don-t-scale)). The goal is to have a small but intensely engaged user base, not a large but indifferent one. This connects to the "well" model described in [[Startup Ideas]] — deep and narrow beats broad and shallow.

## Launch Timing

In "The Hardest Lessons," Graham argues: "I've seen a lot of startups die because they were too slow to release stuff, and none because they were too quick" ([The Hardest Lessons for Startups to Learn](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8w-the-hardest-lessons-for-startups-to-learn)). Big Launches almost never matter. What matters is how happy the initial core of users are, not how many the startup starts with.

The "18 Mistakes" essay lists both slowness in launching (#8) and launching too early (#9) as mistakes, but Graham treats the first as far more dangerous ([The 18 Mistakes That Kill Startups](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8t-the-18-mistakes-that-kill-startups)). Waiting for perfection is a form of procrastination. The [[Founder Mindset]] of shipping fast and iterating is what separates founders who make progress from those who stall.

## Do the Unscalable

Graham describes several patterns of unscalable early work ([Do Things That Don't Scale](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/96-do-things-that-don-t-scale)):

**The Consulting Model.** Pick one user and build as if you are their personal consultant. The mold produces a reusable shape — the custom work reveals what the product should become. This feeds directly into [[Product Development]] by generating real user insight.

**Pulling a Meraki.** Hardware startups can assemble products by hand before investing in factory production. Meraki (YC W06) did this for their first routers.

**Manual-first everything.** Many successful startups started by doing things manually that they later automated. This lets founders learn what users actually need before building the automation.

## Source Talks

| Source | Speaker | Key Point |
|--------|---------|-----------|
| [96-do-things-that-don-t-scale](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/96-do-things-that-don-t-scale) | Paul Graham | Collison Installation, contained fire, consulting model, Meraki |
| [8w-the-hardest-lessons-for-startups-to-learn](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8w-the-hardest-lessons-for-startups-to-learn) | Paul Graham | Release early; startups die from slowness not speed |
| [8t-the-18-mistakes-that-kill-startups](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8t-the-18-mistakes-that-kill-startups) | Paul Graham | Slowness in launching, not getting hands dirty |
| [4D-yc-s-essential-startup-advice](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4D-yc-s-essential-startup-advice) | Y Combinator | Launch fast, talk to users, iterate |

title: Startup Ideas
body:
# Startup Ideas

The YC canon on startup ideas centers on a counterintuitive claim: the way to get startup ideas is not to try to think of them. Graham argues that the verb is "notice," not "think up" ([How to Get Startup Ideas](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8z-how-to-get-startup-ideas)). The best ideas come from founders solving problems they personally experience.

## How to Find Ideas

Graham's core advice is to "live in the future, then build what's missing" ([How to Get Startup Ideas](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8z-how-to-get-startup-ideas)). Founders who work at the frontier of a changing field will naturally encounter problems that are also startup ideas. The key is to train yourself to notice when something is broken or missing, rather than sitting down to brainstorm.

Organic ideas — those that grow from the founder's own experience — are almost always stronger than ideas generated on demand. When a founder has the problem themselves, they have built-in conviction, domain expertise, and an unfakeable understanding of what users need. This deep [[Founder Mindset]] is what separates real insight from surface-level analysis.

## The Well Model

Good startup ideas serve a small number of people who want the thing intensely. Graham calls this a "well" — deep and narrow ([How to Get Startup Ideas](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8z-how-to-get-startup-ideas)). The alternative, a broad shallow hole (many people want it a little), is a much worse starting point. Nearly all good startup ideas have the well shape initially. Facebook started with just Harvard students. Stripe started with just programmers. This principle connects directly to the [[Early-Stage Tactics]] strategy of the "contained fire" — dominate a narrow market before expanding.

## Dangerous Filters

Three unconscious filters cause founders to systematically overlook the best ideas:

**The Schlep Filter.** Founders avoid ideas that involve tedious, messy real-world work. Graham cites Stripe as the canonical example: thousands of programmers saw the payments problem but unconsciously shrank from the schlep of dealing with banks and regulations ([How to Get Startup Ideas](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8z-how-to-get-startup-ideas)).

**The Unsexy Filter.** Founders avoid ideas in boring-seeming domains. The most valuable ideas are often in areas that seem unexciting — infrastructure, logistics, compliance — precisely because few people pursue them.

**"Sitcom" Ideas.** Ideas that sound plausible on paper but nobody actually needs. Graham calls these "made-up" or "sitcom" ideas — the kind of thing a character on a TV show would think of ([Why Smart People Have Bad Ideas](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/91-why-smart-people-have-bad-ideas)). They have a surface-level logic but no genuine demand underneath.

## What Makes a Bad Idea

Graham identifies several patterns in his "18 Mistakes" essay ([The 18 Mistakes That Kill Startups](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8t-the-18-mistakes-that-kill-startups)). Derivative ideas — minor variations on existing products — fail because they lack the conviction and insight that comes from solving a genuine problem. Marginal niches fail because there is no path to a large enough market for [[Growth]]. Ideas pursued without a specific user in mind fail because the founder cannot tell whether they are making progress toward [[Product Development]] milestones.

## Starting With Curiosity

In "Before the Startup," Graham argues that the right preparation is not studying startup mechanics but becoming genuinely expert in a domain ([Before the Startup](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8y-before-the-startup)). At age 20, the optimal strategy is "just learn" — follow curiosity aggressively, because the breadth of knowledge compounds into unexpected startup ideas later.

"Turn your mind into the type that startup ideas form in without any conscious effort," Graham writes. This means cultivating genuine expertise and noticing real problems, not trying to reverse-engineer ideas from market analysis. This advice connects to the broader [[Founder Mindset]] theme that domain expertise matters more than startup expertise.

## Source Talks

| Source | Speaker | Key Point |
|--------|---------|-----------|
| [8z-how-to-get-startup-ideas](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8z-how-to-get-startup-ideas) | Paul Graham | The well model, schlep filter, "live in the future" |
| [8t-the-18-mistakes-that-kill-startups](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8t-the-18-mistakes-that-kill-startups) | Paul Graham | Derivative ideas, marginal niches, no specific user |
| [8y-before-the-startup](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8y-before-the-startup) | Paul Graham | "Just learn," curiosity-first, domain expertise |
| [91-why-smart-people-have-bad-ideas](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/91-why-smart-people-have-bad-ideas) | Paul Graham | Why smart people generate plausible but bad ideas |
